segments.s359-g81: فرستاد is a 3rd-person past tense verb 'he sent/ dispatched' (verb-first word order is common in epic/poetic Persian). هرسو is an adverb 'everywhere/all about' modifying the sending. به کشور is a prepositional phrase 'to/throughout the country/realm' and پیام is the direct object 'a message/tidings' (word order here places adverb and indirect object before the object).
segments.s359-g82: که introduces the content of the message ('that' / 'asking whether'); چون is a comparative 'like/as'. سرو کشمر is a compound name phrase 'the cypress of Kashmar' (سرو = 'cypress', کشمر = the place Kashmar). به گیتی literally 'in the world' (به + گیتی). کدام is an interrogative 'which/which one', completing an indirect question ('asking which in the world is like the cypress of Kashmar').
segments.s359-g83: ز مینو is a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from heaven' (ز = 'from', مینو = 'heaven'). فرستاد again is 3rd-person past 'sent'. زی here is the imperative 'live!' (an archaic/imperative form). من خدای should be read as a vocative/possessive phrase 'my God' or 'O my God' (poetic ordering places من after the noun).
segments.s359-g84: مرا گفت functions as 'he said to me' (مرا = direct object/topic 'to me' with را split in the lemma list; گفت = 'said' — here reported speech follows). زینجا به مینو گرای is an imperative/instruction: زینجا 'from here', به مینو 'toward heaven', گرای is an archaic imperative/verb meaning 'turn/face' (‑گراییدن / to incline/turn); the full phrase means 'from here, turn toward heaven.'
segments.s359-g85: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. هرک is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'whoever' (indefinite relative). این پند من 'this counsel of mine' is the direct object/topic ('this' + 'advice' + possessive 'my'). بشنوید is an imperative/plural present subjunctive 'listen/hear' (بشنو- + -ید), addressing multiple hearers.
segments.s359-g86: پیاده 'on foot' (adverbial). سوی = 'toward'. سرو کشمر = 'the cypress of Kashmar' (place-name with noun). روید here functions as an archaic 2nd- or 3rd-person imperative/hortative 'go' / 'proceed' (classical epic Persian often uses رويد for 'go' / 'advance').
segments.s359-g87: بگیرید is an imperative/formal plural 'take/accept' (گرفتن). پند = 'counsel, advice' (the direct object). ار is an archaic conditional 'if'. دهد is a present/subjunctive or general-present 'gives' (3rd-person). زردهشت = 'Zarthusht' (Zoroaster); the clause reads 'take the counsel if Zoroaster gives it.'
segments.s359-g88: به سوی = 'toward'. بت چین is a compound 'idol of China' (بت = idol, چین = 'China' here). بدارید is an imperative/verb 'keep/put/hold' (دارَدن/داشتن in an archaic imperative form) and پشت is the noun 'back' (meaning 'turn your backs' or 'set your backs toward'); together they mean 'turn your backs toward the Chinese idol' (an instruction to turn away from it).
segments.s359-g33: A coordinated list of social ranks: سران (heads/chiefs) and بزرگان (great men/elders) joined by و; هر مهتران uses هر + plural to mean "every notable/important person." Word order is noun conjunction noun; مهتران is a plural referring to chiefs/notables.
segments.s359-g34: پزشکان is the plural noun "physicians/doctors" followed by the adjectives دانا (learned/wise) and the noun/adjective ناموران (renowned ones). The adjectives follow the noun in typical Persian order and are linked with و.
segments.s359-g35: بر آن جادوی = 'against that magic/sorcery' with بر used in the sense 'against/over'; چارها is the plural 'remedies' and ساختند is perfect past (third-person plural) 'they made/composed'. The whole clause means they prepared remedies directed at that magic.
segments.s359-g36: نه سود آمد is a negated result clause: 'no benefit came' (نه negates سود آمد). از هرچ انداختند literally 'they cast/threw from whatever' — idiomatically 'they tried everything they cast or used.' انداختند is past plural 'they cast/tried'.
segments.s359-g37: پس = 'then/so'. این زردهشت names Zardasht (Zoroaster). پیمبرش is پیمبر + enclitic -ش 'his prophet' (possession marked by -sh). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech).
segments.s359-g38: کزو is a relative/compositional particle 'from which / of which' introducing the clause about دین ایزد ('the religion of God'); نشاید is a negative modal 'it is not proper / ought not' and نهفت is a past-participle/adjective 'hidden.' Together: 'from which the divine religion should not be hidden' → idiomatically, 'the divine religion ought not be concealed.'
segments.s359-g39: که = conjunction 'that/for'. چون ... پذیرد functions as a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if he accepts the religion'; پذیرد is subjunctive/present-tense form 'accepts' (third person). ز روز نخست is a temporal phrase 'from the first day' with poetic preposition ز = از.
segments.s359-g40: شود رسته از درد = شود (become/will be) + رسته (freed/released) + از درد (from pain) meaning 'he will be freed from pain.' و گردد درست links with و + گردد (becomes) + درست (right/healthy) meaning 'and will become well/proper.' Both شوند/گردد are stative/result verbs in third person.
segments.s359-g9: This clause is indirect speech marker + addressee + reporting verb + self‑identification: به (preposition 'to/for') introduces the addressee, شاه کیان is a compound title 'the Kayan king' (شاه + the dynastic name کیان). گفت is the past tense reporting verb 'said' (here used to introduce the prophet's words). پیغمبرم is 'my/your prophet' or simply 'the prophet' with the possessive -م ('my') functioning as a vocative/self‑identifying tag.
segments.s359-g10: This is the quoted content: سوی تو = 'toward you / to you' (سوی + pronoun تو); خرد رهنمون is a noun phrase 'wisdom (خرد) [and] guidance (رهنمون)' with a compact apposition; آورم is a 1st person present/subjunctive/archaic-form verb meaning 'I bring / I will bring' (here the speaker promises to bring guidance). Word order places the direction (سوی تو) before the theme (خرد رهنمون) and ends with the verb آورم, typical of Persian verbal sentences.
segments.s359-g11: جهان‌آفرین is an epithet 'the World‑Creator' (creator of the world). گفت again is the reporting verb (see above) introducing the direct commands. بپذیر دین is an imperative clause: بپذیر is the (archaic/poetic) imperative/subjunctive 'accept' and دین is its object 'the faith / religion'. The two verbs after گفت are imperatives issuing instruction.
segments.s359-g12: نگه کن is a compound imperative 'keep/observe/hold (it)' formed by noun نگه plus the auxiliary imperative کن. بر این آسمان و زمین is a prepositional phrase: بر = 'upon/on', این (here written in two pieces as بر + ین) = 'these/this', آسمان و زمین = 'heaven(s) and earth'. Together the clause commands: 'keep it upon these heavens and the earth' (i.e., preserve/establish the faith over heaven and earth).
segments.s359-g13: This subordinate clause begins with که 'that/for/because' introducing an explanatory clause. بی‌خاک literally 'without earth/soil' (بی- = without + خاک), و links 'and', آبش = آب + enclitic -ش 'its water' ('its water' / 'and its water'). برآورده‌ام is a perfective 1st‑person verb 'I have brought forth / I have produced' (classical phrasing meaning 'I made/created it').
segments.s359-g14: نگه کن again is an imperative 'look / behold' (نگه + کن). بدو = به + او/ــش 'to it / toward it' (directional pronoun), تاش = 'effort, endeavor' and functions as the object of attention, چون کرده‌ام = 'how I have done (it)' with چون = 'how' and کرده‌ام = perfect 'I have done'. The whole clause means 'look toward it (observe) the effort, how I have done it.' Note the inverted poetic word order (imperative + direction + object + subordinate clause).
segments.s359-g15: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'behold!'. تا here functions in a concessive/limiting sense ('see whether / even') introducing a clause of capability: تواند = 'can / is able' (3rd singular), چنین = 'such / thus', کرد = past 'did/made'. کس = 'anyone' (the clause is asserting that no one else can do it). In modern phrasing: 'Look — see if anyone can do such a thing.'
segments.s359-g16: مگر introduces an exception 'except'. من = 'I', که = relative 'who / that', هستم = 'I am', جهاندار is an honorific 'lord/sovereign of the world' (epithet), و بس = 'and enough' / 'and that suffices' (a closing emphatic phrase). The clause means 'except me, who am the world‑lord — and that is sufficient.'
segments.s359-g25: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. بشنید is simple past (he heard); the subject شاه follows the verb in this poetic order. ازو = 'from him' marks the source of what was heard. به دین به places 'dīn' (religion) as the thing received/accepted; the second به is a poetic/directional particle anticipating the coming verb and marks the adoption/turning toward that religion.
segments.s359-g26: پذیرفت is past tense 'he accepted' (transitive). ازو again gives the source 'from him'. راه و آیین are coordinated objects ('way and rites'). The final به is a directional/poetic particle reinforcing that he took/adopted those things (common in Ferdowsi's compressed word order).
segments.s359-g27: نبرده is a negative perfect/past meaning 'has/had not taken' (negation fused with the verb). برادرش is 'his brother' (برادر + possessive -ش) and is followed by the proper-name apposition فرخ زریر naming that brother.
segments.s359-g28: کجا introduces a question 'where?'. ژنده modifies پیل to give 'ragged/tattered elephant' (adjective before noun). آوریدی is 2nd-person past 'you brought'. به زیر is a locative phrase 'to/beneath' (به + زیر) indicating where the elephant was brought — word order is poetically compact.
segments.s359-g29: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among'. شاهان is the plural 'kings'; شه here functions as an emphatic/title 'the king' (appositive). پیر گشته is a compound verbal phrase: پیر (old) + گشته (become/has turned), i.e. 'has grown old'. به بلخ is the locative 'in/to Balkh'.
segments.s359-g30: جهان is the subject 'the world'. بر دل ریش او is a prepositional phrase 'upon his heart and beard' (بر + two nouns + possessive او). گشته تلخ is the predicate 'has become bitter' (گشته = become; تلخ = bitter), so literally 'the world has become bitter upon his heart and beard'.
segments.s359-g31: شده (شده) is perfect/passive-form 'has become' and heads a series of coordinated adjectival predicates: زار 'wretched', بیمار 'sick/ill', بی‌هوش 'unconscious/senseless'. توش (تو + ش) means 'in him/inside him' and links these states to his inner condition.
segments.s359-g32: به نزدیک او = 'near him' (به + نزدیک + pronoun). زهر مانند نوش is an inverted noun phrase 'a drink like poison' (زهر 'poison' + مانند 'like' + نوش 'drink'); the verb is omitted because this phrase complements the prior clause (i.e. 'there was/it was like a drink of poison near him').
segments.s359-g57: نبشتی is a poetic past-form of the verb نبشتن and here functions like a passive/impersonal 'was inscribed' or 'one inscribed'; it is followed by a prepositional phrase بر زاد (بر + زاد) meaning 'on the birth/of the occasion of the birth'. سرو سهی is a noun phrase (cypress + poetic epithet) where سهی is an attributive epithet attached to سرو; classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe in writing.
segments.s359-g58: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. پذرفت is a simple past 'accepted' (transitive). گشتاسپ is the subject/name of the clause; دین بهی is the object phrase 'the Good (i.e. Best) Religion' with بهی as an adjectival epithet meaning 'good' or 'excellent'.
segments.s359-g59: گوا کرد is a compound where گوا ('witness') + کرد ('did/made') yields 'made (as) witness / bore witness'. مر is an enclitic meaning 'to me' (dative), and سرو آزاد را is the noun phrase 'the noble cypress' with را marking it as the definite/affected topic. Word order places the verb first for poetic effect, with the dative and object following.
segments.s359-g60: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' introducing the result/continuation. گستراند is past tense 'spread/expanded' (he spread). خرد is the object 'wisdom' and داد here is taken as the verbal 'gave' (not the noun 'justice'); the final را marks the object (poetic placement puts object marker after داد). The clause therefore means 'thus he spread wisdom and gave (it)'.
segments.s359-g61: چو چندی functions as a temporal clause 'when / after a little while'. برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/appeared' and, with the following compound برین سالیان ('over these years' = بر + ین (orthographic 'این') + سالیان), indicates the lapse of several years: 'when some years had arisen/elapsed'.
segments.s359-g62: This clause names the cypress and states its change: مران is treated here as a proper name or identifier (the Merān cypress), سرو is the noun, and گشتش is the past of گشتن with the enclitic -ش 'it/him' meaning 'it became/turned'. استبر is an older/rare verb used here in the sense of 'be sought/desired' (poetic usage). میان 'among / in the midst' gives the domain where it became so.
segments.s359-g63: چنان گشت is a resultative construction 'thus/so it became'. آزاد سرو بلند puts the epithet آزاد and the noun سرو together with the adjective بلند 'tall' — literally 'became (a) noble, tall cypress'. Word order (adjectives both before and after the noun) is flexible in epic Persian for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.s359-g64: که introduces the result clause 'so that...'. برگرد here is used in the sense 'to turn/encircle (around)', او is the pronoun 'him/it', and برنگشتی is the past form 'did not turn/was not turned' (poetic phrasing stacks verb forms and subject/object). کمند 'lasso/noose' is the agent/subject in sense: the idea is 'so tall that the noose could not turn/encircle him' (i.e. no snare could encompass him).
segments.s359-g89: به is the preposition 'for/unto' showing purpose or dedication. برز و فر is a paired compound meaning 'highness/exaltation and glory' (a formulaic phrase), followed by شاه ایرانیان where شاه + ایرانیان is a genitive relation ('the king of the Iranians'); the whole phrase yields 'for the majesty and glory of the Iranian king.'
segments.s359-g90: ببندید is an imperative (2pl) 'fasten/tie up' (classical Persian imperative form). کشتی is the noun 'ships' and is followed by the quantifier همه ('all'), a post-nominal quantifier here meaning 'all the ships'. بر میان is a locative phrase: بر 'on/upon' + میان 'middle' → 'in the midst' or 'at midstream,' giving 'fasten all the ships in the middle.'
segments.s359-g91: در is the preposition 'in'. آیین پیشینیان is a genitive (آیین 'rite/ceremony' + پیشینیان 'the ancients/ancestors') meaning 'the rites of the ancestors.' منگرید is a prohibitive jussive 'do not look/do not stare' (2pl negative imperative), instructing avoidance of gawking.
segments.s359-g92: برین is a fused form of بر + این ('on/over/at this') used demonstratively: 'this (over)'. سایه سروبن is a noun phrase 'the shade of the cypress' (سروبن 'cypress-trunk' post-modifies سایه). بگذرید is the imperative (2pl) 'pass (by/through)'; together: 'pass beneath/through this cypress's shade.'
segments.s359-g93: سوی 'toward' introduces the direction. گنبد آذر is a genitive: گنبد 'dome' + آذر 'fire' (here a reference to the fire or the fire-temple), so 'the dome of (the) fire.' آرید is an imperative (2pl) meaning 'turn/bring' (classical verb form), and روی 'face' is the direct object: 'turn your face toward the dome of fire.'
segments.s359-g94: به here means 'by/at' indicating agency/instrument ('by the command of ...'). فرمان 'command' takes the following nominal phrase پیغمبر راست‌گوی 'the truthful Prophet' (پیغمبر 'prophet' + راست‌گوی 'truth-telling', an epithet). The whole phrase means 'by the command of the truthful Prophet.'
segments.s359-g95: پراگنده is an adjective/participle 'widespread' placed before فرمانش ('his command'); اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in/throughout the world' (اندر = 'in/within', جهان = 'world'). The phrase reads 'his widely-spread command in the world.'
segments.s359-g96: سوی 'toward' repeats for two coordinated targets: نامداران 'the renowned/illustrious (ones)' and مهان 'the guests/visitors'. The repetition of سوی emphasizes the command reaching each group: 'toward the famed and toward the guests.'
segments.s359-g49: پس is a discourse adverb meaning “then.” آزاده (adjective before the name) qualifies گشتاسپ: literary word order places the epithet before the proper name, so “آزاده گشتاسپ” = “the noble Gushasp.” برشد is an archaic/synonymic past-form meaning “rose, stood up” (3rd sg. past). به گاه forms a temporal phrase “at the time / at that moment,” with به as the preposition and گاه “time.”
segments.s359-g50: فرستاد is the simple past “he sent / dispatched” (3rd sg.). هرسو is an adverb meaning “everywhere / in every direction.” به کشور is a prepositional phrase “into/through the realm,” where به is the preposition and کشور the noun “realm/country.” سپاه is the direct object “army, host.” Word order is typical epic Persian: verb (فرستاد) with its adverbial and object phrases around it.
segments.s359-g51: پراگنده is an adjective/adverb “widespread/scattered.” اندر جهان is a locative phrase “in/throughout the world,” where اندر = “in/within” and جهان = “world.” موبدان (mōbed-plural) is the noun ‘priests’; here the phrase describes their distribution (i.e. “the mōbeds were spread throughout the world”).
segments.s359-g52: نهاد here is the 3rd-person past of نهادن “to place/establish” (= “he placed/established”). از بر is an older compound prepositional expression meaning roughly “upon / on top of / from upon,” with از = “from/of” and بر = “on/upon.” آذران گنبدان is a genitival/attributive combination referring to the domed structures associated with the Azarān (literally “Azaran domes”); the whole segment says he set/installed (them) upon those domes. The object (the priests or fires) is supplied from the context.
segments.s359-g53: نخست is the adverb “first / initially.” آذر مهربرزین is a named fire (noun + proper name), with آذر “fire” followed by the name مهربرزین; Persian often places the noun before the proper name. نهاد is again the 3rd‑person past “he placed/established.” The segment reads “First he placed the fire Mehrbarzin.”
segments.s359-g54: به کشمر نگر is an exhortation/imperative phrase: به + place (کشمر, Kashmar) plus نگِر (imperative of نگریستن/نگه داشتن) meaning “look toward Kashmar” or “turn your eyes to Kashmar.” تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning “so that / in order to,” and چه آیین نهاد is a free indirect question-clause “what ritual he established,” with آیین “ritual/ceremony” and نهاد the past verb “he established.”
segments.s359-g55: یکی is the indefinite “a certain / one.” سرو آزاده is the noun + epithet “a noble cypress,” with the adjective preceding the noun in this poetic order. بود is the past of “to be” (here: “there was / it was”), and از بهشت is the source phrase “from Paradise.” Together this gives “There was a certain noble cypress from Paradise.”
segments.s359-g56: به پیش = “before / in front of”; در آذر = “in/at the fire” (آذر = fire or the fire‑shrine). آن را uses آن + the object marker را to mark the cypress as direct object (“it”). بکشت is an archaic/past verbal form here meaning “he made/caused/placed it” (literary usage of the root given as بک-). The segment reads literally “he placed/caused it before the fire.”
segments.s359-g73: فریدونش is the proper name فریدون with the enclitic -ش meaning "his" (classical/poetic possessive). را is the definite direct‑object marker attached to that phrase. نیز (= also) adds that this action is in addition to others. با + گاوسار indicates accompaniment: "with Gausar." The whole chunk is the definite object phrase ("his Fereydun, also, with Gausar") that will be governed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s359-g74: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning "he ordered/commanded." کردن here functions as a light verb (auxiliary) forming a causative or periphrastic construction ("had X done / ordered X to be done"). برانجا (ب + آن‌جا) gives the destination/ place "to/there." نگار is a noun meaning "picture/ornament/inscription" here (i.e. something to be made or put there). Together: "he ordered (that) [them] be sent/placed there (for the engraving/ornament)."
segments.s359-g75: همه modifies مهتران and means "all/everyone." مهتران (poetic plural) refers to the great men/chiefs/elders (here rendered by a sense meaning "great"). را is the definite object marker, marking those chiefs as the direct objects. بر آن‌جا gives the location "upon/to that place," and نگاشت is the past tense verb "wrote/inscribed." Syntactically this is an object phrase followed by a locative phrase and a past verb: "(he) inscribed all the chiefs there."
segments.s359-g76: نگر is an imperative interjection meaning "look! / behold." تا here introduces the ensuing result/clause (roughly "— so that / — behold" used as an intensifier). چنان is the demonstrative/adverb "such/so," modifying کامگاری "success/fulfillment." که introduces the relative/result clause and داشت is the past tense "he had/experienced." The clause as a whole commands the reader's attention: "Look — such success as he had."
segments.s359-g77: چو is the temporal conjunction "when." نیکو شد is the predicate "became beautiful/ excellent" (نیکو = good/beautiful; شد = became). آن نامور کاخ زر is a noun phrase: آن = "that," نامور = "renowned/famed" modifying کاخ "palace," and زر = "gold," i.e. "that famed palace of gold/ golden palace." Word order follows classical Persian patterns (modifiers around the noun).
segments.s359-g78: به دیوارها = toward/on the walls (به used here to mark the target/location). بر + نشانده forms the locative/positional phrase "placed upon" (بر with نشانده gives the idea of placing on/upon). نشانده is the past participle/agentive form meaning "placed/seated," and گهر = "gem/jewel." The phrase therefore reads: "(he) placed jewels upon the walls." Note the poetic inversion: the locative phrase precedes the verb-participle.
segments.s359-g79: به گردش literally "by/with a turn" (به here indicates manner, "by/with"). یکی باره = "one occasion/at one stroke" (یکی = one; باره = instance/occasion), giving the sense "in a single turn/at one blow." کرد is the past tense main verb "did/made," and آهنین (adjective) describes what was made as "iron/iron‑bound." Together the clause means roughly "he made (it) iron in a single turn" or "by one iron turn he effected it."
segments.s359-g80: نشست here is used as "seat/setting" or the action "to sit/take a seat." اندرو = "in it/therein" (locative). کرد is the light/past verb again ("he did / he took"), and شاه زمین = "the king of the earth/land" (شاه = king; زمین = earth/land). The word order is inverted (locative + action before the verb), so the sense is "the king of the land sat there / took his seat in it."
segments.s359-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. یک چند سالان is an archaic quantifying time-phrase meaning 'a few years' (literally 'one several years') and functions as the time adjunct. برآمد here is a past intransitive verb 'came/rose up', and بر + ین (written together as برین) literally 'upon this' but functions here as part of the verbal-frame giving the sense 'had come upon/elapsed'; the whole segment signals 'when a few years had gone by'.
segments.s359-g2: درختی is an indefinite subject 'a tree'. پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'appeared / came into view' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'). اندر زمین is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within the earth/ground'. Word order is subject — verb — locative, typical of classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s359-g3: در ایوان گشتاسپ is a locative phrase 'in the iwan (porch) of Gushtasp' (در + noun + name). بر سوی کاخ is another locative/directional phrase 'on the side toward the palace' (بر سوی = 'on the side of / toward'). Together they fix the tree's position relative to the palace.
segments.s359-g4: درختی is the indefinite subject 'a tree'. گشن is an attributive adjective; in classical/poetic usage it qualifies the tree (here rendered as 'bushy/abundant' in sense) and is followed by بود, the past copula 'was'. بسیار شاخ is a noun phrase 'very many branches' where بسیار modifies شاخ ('many branches'). The whole clause states 'there was a tree, (gashn), with very many branches.'
segments.s359-g5: همه برگ وی is 'all its leaves' (همه = 'all', برگ = 'leaf', وی = 'its/it' referring to the tree). پند و بارش خرد is a nominal predication with an omitted copula: 'pnd and its burden wisdom' — i.e. 'its leaves were counsel and its load/fruit was wisdom' (بارش = بار + ش, 'its burden'). Persian often omits the verb 'to be' in such nominal statements.
segments.s359-g6: This is a rhetorical/relative clause. کسی is the indefinite 'anyone' or 'someone', followed by a relative/linking particle (کو here functioning in older usage), خرد پرورد is a verbal adjectival phrase 'who nurtured/raised wisdom' (خرد = 'wisdom', پرورد = 'nurtured'), and کی مرد frames the question 'who is the man?' — together: 'who is the man that could/does nurture wisdom?'. The clause is rhetorical and expects that nurturing wisdom is rare or noteworthy.
segments.s359-g7: خجسته پی is a short nominal phrase 'auspicious lineage/ancestry' (خجسته = 'auspicious/blessed', پی here used poetically for 'lineage/descent'). و نام او زردهشت is 'and his name (was) Zardesht' — Persian commonly omits the copula 'was' in such appositive phrases, so the sense is 'he had an auspicious descent and his name was Zardesht.'
segments.s359-g8: که is a relative 'who/whom' linking back to the named person. آهرمن is the agent 'Ahriman' (the evil spirit). بدکنش here names an evil agent/ill-doer (literally 'bad-doer') and را is the definite object marker; بکشت is the past verb 'killed/slain' (archaic form). Together: 'whom Ahriman's evil agent killed' / 'he was slain by Ahriman's evil agent.'
segments.s359-g41: This is a short nominal list: 'شهنشاه' (Shahanshah) is the first noun, joined by the coordinating 'و' (and). 'زین پس' is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'from now on / thereafter' (here treated as a unit), and 'زریر سوار' names a person with an epithet: proper name 'زریر' followed by the descriptive noun 'سوار' (rider). There is no copula; the whole phrase lists actors who will appear in the clause that follows.
segments.s359-g42: 'همه' (everyone/all) is the subject; 'دین پذیرنده' is a noun phrase built with the participle 'پذیرنده' (acceptor/one who accepts) modifying 'دین' (the faith) — i.e. 'those accepting the faith'. 'از شهریار' uses 'از' to mark source/agent ('from the king'). The whole phrase states that all accepted the religion from the sovereign; participial adjective is used instead of a finite relative clause.
segments.s359-g43: 'همه' (everyone) is the subject; 'سوی شاه زمین' is a prepositional/ directional phrase meaning 'toward the king of the land' (سوی = toward, شاه زمین = 'king of the land' with زمین qualifying شاه). 'آمدند' is the past plural verb 'they came.' Word order is S + adverbial phrase + V, a normal presentation in narrative.
segments.s359-g44: This line contains two coordinated actions. 'ببستند کشتی' is a verb + object (poetic inversion) meaning 'they moored/tied up the ship(s)' — 'ببستند' is a past plural verb. The second clause 'به دین آمدند' is an idiomatic construction where 'به' marks motion into a state and 'آمدند' (they came) means 'they embraced/converted to the faith.' The sequence describes arrival, mooring ships, then conversion.
segments.s359-g45: 'پدید آمد' is a two-word verbal expression (past) meaning 'appeared/manifested.' 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that/the,' and 'فره ایزدی' is a compound: 'فره' (farr, the divinely-granted radiance/glory) qualified by the adjective 'ایزدی' (divine). Word order here is verb + demonstrative + noun phrase, a poetic inversion to emphasize the manifestation.
segments.s359-g46: The verb 'برفت' here is an archaic/prefixal past of 'رفتن' meaning 'departed/was driven away' (an impersonal or intransitive past). 'از دل' marks source ('from the heart(s)'); 'بد سگالان' is a genitival phrase where 'بد' (bad/evil) qualifies the proper name 'سگالان' (Sagalān), i.e. 'the wicked Sagalān'; 'بدی' is the noun 'evil' that left. The order (verb followed by source phrase and the victim) is poetic inversion.
segments.s359-g47: 'پر از نور مینو' is an adjectival phrase 'full of the light of heaven' (پر = full (of); از introduces the source 'نور مینو' = 'heavenly light'). 'ببد' is an archaic past/copular verb meaning 'became/was' and precedes the subject 'دخمه‌ها' (the tombs), so the clause reads 'the tombs became full of heavenly light' with verb–subject order for poetic effect.
segments.s359-g48: 'وز' here is an archaic variant/poetic form functioning like 'از' ('from') marking separation from impurity (not one of the listed senses, so marked -1). 'آلودگی' is 'defilement/pollution'; 'پاک شد' is a two-word predicate 'became clean/pure' (پاک + شد); 'تخمه‌ها' is the subject placed at the end for emphasis and means 'the offspring/descendants' (or 'seedlings' depending on context). The clause means 'the offspring were cleansed of defilement.'
segments.s359-g17: گر is the conditional particle 'if' introducing the clause. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic adverbial particle meaning 'now/by now' and sits immediately after گر. دانی is 2nd‑person singular present 'you know' and takes the following clause as its content via the complementizer که. The clause من کردم این (literally 'I did this') uses a poetic, slightly inverted word order: subject من before the past verb کردم and the demonstrative/ direct object این after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s359-g18: مرا ... را is the object 'me' with the definite/object marker را attached (here shown split). In this line the object precedes the verb for emphasis. باید is the modal 'must/ought' that governs the verbal form خواند (3rd person singular 'he/it calls/summons'); poetic word order places the verb + modal before the subject. جهان‌آفرین is the subject 'the World‑Creator/Creator' and is placed at the end (typical in verse), so the whole means 'the Creator must call me.'
segments.s359-g19: ز (poetic for از) is the preposition 'from' and governs گوینده 'the speaker', which is placed before the imperative بپذیر 'accept'. The phrase به دین اوی literally 'into/with his religion' uses به to mark direction/target ('into' or 'toward') and اوی as the 3rd‑person possessive 'his' following the noun.
segments.s359-g20: بیاموز is an imperative 'learn!' with its source marked by ازو 'from him'. راه و آیین are coordinated direct objects ('way/manner and rites/practices'); the coordinating ו links them and the possessive اوی follows, applying to both nouns (i.e. 'his way and his rites'). Word order places the verb first (imperative) then the source and objects.
segments.s359-g21: نگر is an imperative 'look!/observe!'. تا here introduces a purposive/result clause ('so that' / 'in order to') and combines with چه گوید 'what he says' (چه as interrogative 'what', گوید as 3rd‑person verb 'he says'); بران 'thereon/thereupon' links that clause to the following imperative. کار کن is a second imperative 'do the deed' (کار as 'deed' placed before the imperative کن). The sequence directs the addressee to observe speech and then act accordingly.
segments.s359-g22: This segment contains two imperatives: خرد برگزین 'choose wisdom' — here خرد 'wisdom' is the object placed before the verb برگزین (choose) — and این جهان خوار کن 'make/despise this world'. In the latter, این جهان 'this world' is the object and خوار کن is a causative/imperative construction 'make (it) despised' (i.e. 'despise the world' or 'regard the world as contemptible'). The repeated کن is the verb 'do/make' used in imperatives.
segments.s359-g23: بیاموز is the imperative 'learn!'. آیین و دین are the coordinated nouns 'rites/ceremony and religion' serving as the direct object(s). بهی is an archaic adjective meaning 'good/excellent' placed after the nouns (postpositive adjective), so 'آیین و دین بهی' = 'good rites and religion.'
segments.s359-g24: که here is the subordinating particle 'for/that' introducing the explanatory clause. بی‌دین 'without religion/godless' is the subject of the clause, ناخوب 'not good/unseemly' is the predicate adjective, and باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here as the copula. مهی is a final predicate/adjective (here 'beautiful' in its sense), so the clause says roughly 'for the faithless are not beautiful/are unbecoming.'
segments.s359-g97: همه نامداران is a noun phrase with همه (’all/every’) before the plural noun نامداران (’renowned ones’). به فرمان اوی is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘by/at his command’; به here means ‘by/at’ and اوی is an archaic possessive pronoun ‘his’ attached after فرمان (word order: NP + prep phrase).
segments.s359-g98: سوی is the preposition ‘toward’; سرو کشمر is the noun phrase ‘the cypress of Kashmar’ (کشمر is a place-name). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/set/turn’, and روی (’face’) is the thing they set — the idiom نهادنِ روی = ‘to turn one’s face toward’. So literal order: ‘toward [the cypress of Kashmar] placed-they face.’
segments.s359-g99: پرستشکده گشت is ‘the sanctuary became’ (گشت = became, past). زان سان که is a fixed connective ‘so/so that’ (زان = ‘from that’; سان = ‘such/like’) introducing the subordinate clause that follows. پشت here is a noun ‘back/behind’ that will be governed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s359-g100: ببست is the classical past tense (3rd singular) of بستن ‘to close/bind’ (here ‘bound/shut’). اندرو = اندر + و (’in/within it’) marks location ‘within/inside’. دیو را uses را as the definite object marker before the object ‘the demon’. زردهشت (Zerdusht/Zoroaster) follows the object as agent in poetic inversion; altogether: ‘Zoroaster bound the demon within it.’
segments.s359-g101: بهشتیش خوان is ‘the banquet of Paradise’ with the enclitic ش on بهشت forming a possessive ‘of Paradise’ and خوان meaning ‘banquet’. ار is the archaic conditional ‘if’. ندانی is the 2nd-person present negative/archaic form ‘you do not know’ (دانستن), and همی is an emphatic particle (‘indeed/ever’). The clause therefore reads ‘If you do not know the banquet of Paradise…’.
segments.s359-g102: چرا introduces the question ‘why’. سرو کشمرش is the cypress of Kashmar, with ش on کشمر indicating possession. خوانی is a present form of خواندن used here in the sense ‘to call/name’ (’you call’), and همی again is emphatic. The clause asks ‘why do you call the cypress of Kashmar a “banquet”?’
segments.s359-g103: چراکش is a noun (here used as a name/epithet; lexically glossed as ‘herdsman/pasturer’). نخوانی is a present/archaic verbal form of خواندن meaning ‘you (do not) call’ in this rhetorical question (the initial ن expresses the negative/optative nuance in older idiom). نهال بهشت is the direct object ‘sapling of Paradise’ (نهال + genitive بهشت).
segments.s359-g104: که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that’. شاه کیانش is ‘the king of the Kians’ (کیان + possessive ش). به کشمر is a locative phrase ‘in/into Kashmar’ (به = ‘into/in’ here), and بکشت is the archaic past tense (prefix ب‑ + root کشت) meaning ‘planted/caused to grow’ in context. Together: ‘that the Kian king planted in Kashmar.’
segments.s359-g65: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بسیار modifies the following verbal/nominal idea and here means 'many' (many leaves). برگشت is used poetically for the tree putting forth/turning (leaves/foliage) — this meaning is not among the provided senses, so treat it as an archaic/poetic verb of growth; و simply links the two parallel phrases; the second بسیار again means 'many' and شاخ is the noun 'branches'.
segments.s359-g66: بکرد is an archaic past-tense form (b- prefix + کرد) meaning 'he made/caused' (3rd person). از is the preposition 'from/out of', بر here functions spatially ('by/on/near') with او 'it/him' as the reference point; یکی خوب کاخ is the direct object: یکی 'one/a single', خوب 'beautiful', and کاخ 'palace'. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and the object follows it.
segments.s359-g67: This is a compact dimensional clause. چهل is the numeral 'forty' (implicit in the segment); رش is a measure/word here (sense list lacks the precise unit) and is given as a small quantity sense in the inventory. به بالا is a directional phrase 'upwards' (به + بالا), and پهنا 'breadth' states the transverse measure. The conjunction و links the height and breadth statements.
segments.s359-g68: نکرد is the past negative of کردن ('did not do' / 'there was not'); in context it functions to say the thing did not lack or to state what was inside. از is the preposition 'from/of', بنه here refers to the pistachio (or pistachio-wood/elements) and اندرو (archaic compound of اندر + رو) means 'within' or 'inside it'. آب و گل list the contents 'water and flowers'. Overall the clause describes what lay inside the structure.
segments.s359-g69: دو ایوان 'two iwans/porches' is the object phrase. برآورد is a past verb here meaning 'he raised/caused to be built' (the second listed sense 'to accomplish/fulfil' fits this poetic usage). از زر پاک is a prepositional phrase 'out of/pure gold'—از marks the material, زر 'gold', پاک 'pure'. Word order: object + verb + material phrase.
segments.s359-g70: زمینش is 'its ground/floor' (زمین + possessive -ش). ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'of/from' and introduces the material: سیم 'silver'. و links the next material phrase; the second ز likewise introduces عنبرش 'its amber/ambergris', and خاک 'soil/dust' names the surface/substrate. The clause therefore states the materials of the ground/floor.
segments.s359-g71: برو is an imperative 'go'. بر here is the preposition 'on/upon' marking where the inscription is to be placed. نگارید (from نگاشت/نگاریدن) functions here in the sense of inscription/depiction — the poetic verb/noun form signaling 'inscribe/write/depict' — and جمشید را is the direct object (جمشید with the object marker را). The sequence gives an imperative instruction to inscribe Jamshid on (it).
segments.s359-g72: پرستنده is a participial noun 'worshipper/devotee'. مر is the archaic/object form meaning 'me/my' (i.e., 'my worshipper'). ماه و خورشید 'the moon and the sun' are the objects of veneration; را is the definite object/topic marker attached at the end. Grammatically this fragment names Jamshid as 'my worshipper of the moon and the sun.'

sentences.s359-s41: He sent a message everywhere through the realm, asking which in the world is like the cypress of Kashmar.
sentences.s359-s42: From heaven he sent, 'Live, my God'; he said to me, 'From here, turn toward heaven.'
sentences.s359-s43: Now, whoever heeds this counsel of mine, on foot go toward the cypress of Kashmar.
sentences.s359-s44: Take the counsel if Zarthusht (Zoroaster) gives it; turn your backs toward the idol of China.
sentences.s359-s17: The chiefs and great men and every notable, and the learned and renowned physicians.
sentences.s359-s18: They made remedies against that sorcery; no benefit came from whatever they cast/tried.
sentences.s359-s19: Then this Zardasht, his prophet, said that the religion of God ought not to be hidden.
sentences.s359-s20: For when he accepts the religion from the very first day, he will be freed from pain and become well.
sentences.s359-s5: The prophet said to the Kayan king, "I will bring wisdom and guidance to you."
sentences.s359-s6: The World‑Creator said, "Accept the faith; keep it upon these heavens and the earth."
sentences.s359-s7: For I brought it forth without earth and its water; behold and see the effort with which I have done it.
sentences.s359-s8: Behold — see whether anyone could do such a thing, except me, who am the lord of the world; that suffices.
sentences.s359-s13: When the king heard this from him, he accepted his religion and adopted his way and rites.
sentences.s359-s14: His brother, Farrokh Zarrir, had not taken it; where did you bring the ragged elephant beneath?
sentences.s359-s15: From among kings the king grew old in Balkh; the world had become bitter upon his heart and beard.
sentences.s359-s16: He had become wretched, ill and senseless; near him there was a drink like poison.
sentences.s359-s29: Upon the birth of that noble cypress was inscribed that Gushtasp accepted the Good Religion.
sentences.s359-s30: The noble cypress testified to me; thus he spread wisdom and gave (it).
sentences.s359-s31: When some years had gone by, the Merān cypress grew to be sought among (others).
sentences.s359-s32: Thus the noble cypress became so tall that no lasso could encircle him.
sentences.s359-s45: Fasten all the ships in the middle for the majesty and glory of the Iranian king.
sentences.s359-s46: In the rites of the ancestors do not stare; pass beneath this cypress's shade.
sentences.s359-s47: Turn your faces toward the dome of fire; by the command of the truthful Prophet.
sentences.s359-s48: His command, widely spread throughout the world, was carried toward the renowned and toward the guests.
sentences.s359-s25: Then the noble Gushasp rose up at that moment and dispatched an army in every direction across the realm.
sentences.s359-s26: He spread the mōbeds widely through the world and placed (them) upon the Azaran domes.
sentences.s359-s27: First he placed the fire Mehrbarzin; look toward Kashmar to see what ritual he established.
sentences.s359-s28: A certain noble cypress came from Paradise; he placed it before the fire.
sentences.s359-s37: He likewise had his Fereydun sent there, together with Gausar.
sentences.s359-s38: He had all the chiefs inscribed there; look — how great a success he enjoyed.
sentences.s359-s39: When that famed golden palace became beautiful, he set jewels upon its walls.
sentences.s359-s40: With a single iron turn he made it; the king of the earth took his seat there.
sentences.s359-s1: When a few years had passed, a tree sprang up out of the earth.
sentences.s359-s2: In Gushtasp's iwan, on the side toward the palace, there stood a tree with very many branches.
sentences.s359-s3: All its leaves were counsel, and its burden (fruit) was wisdom — who is the man that could nurture wisdom?
sentences.s359-s4: His lineage was auspicious and his name was Zardesht; he was slain by Ahriman's evil agent.
sentences.s359-s21: The Shahanshah, and thereafter Zareer the rider, all accepted the faith from the king.
sentences.s359-s22: Everyone came to the king of the land; they moored the ships and embraced the faith.
sentences.s359-s23: That divine farr (heavenly radiance) appeared; evil departed from the hearts of the wicked Sagalān.
sentences.s359-s24: The tombs became full of heavenly light; the offspring were cleansed of defilement.
sentences.s359-s9: If you now know that I did this, the World‑Creator must call me.
sentences.s359-s10: Accept from the speaker his religion; learn from him his way and rites.
sentences.s359-s11: Watch what he says and do that deed; choose wisdom and despise this world.
sentences.s359-s12: Learn the good rites and religion, for the faithless are unbecoming.
sentences.s359-s49: All the renowned men, at his command, turned their faces toward the cypress of Kashmar.
sentences.s359-s50: The sanctuary became such that Zoroaster bound the demon within it.
sentences.s359-s51: If you do not know the heavenly banquet, why do you call the cypress of Kashmar a banquet?
sentences.s359-s52: Why do you not call it Charākash, the sapling of Paradise which the king of the Kians planted in Kashmar?
sentences.s359-s33: When it put forth many leaves and many branches, he built beside it a single beautiful palace.
sentences.s359-s34: It rose forty 'rash' high and was forty in breadth; inside it were pistachio, water, and flowers.
sentences.s359-s35: He raised two iwans of pure gold; its floor was of silver and its dust (ground) of amber.
sentences.s359-s36: Go and inscribe on it: 'Jamshid, my worshipper of the moon and the sun.'
